items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs do not change with output level (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change in proportion to output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs are paid every month and variable costs are paid weekly.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are the cheapest costs and variable costs are the most expensive.
items.why: The distinction is about behaviour with output, not payment frequency. Rent is fixed because producing more units doesn't increase it; flour for a bakery is variable because more loaves means more flour.

items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.

items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.

items.q: Splitting a market by lifestyle and values is which type of segmentation?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Psychographic. “Demographic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which life-cycle stage typically has the LOWEST profits but rising sales?
items.why: Correct answer: Introduction. “Growth” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many stages are in the standard product life cycle?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
